pubmed_links: [Spieler et al. (2014) Restless legs syndrome-associated intronic common variant in Meis1 alters enhancer function in the developing telencephalon](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24642863/); [Salminen et al. (2019) Role of MEIS1 in restless legs syndrome: From GWAS to functional studies in mice](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31229170/); [Kittke et al. (2024) RLS-associated MEIS transcription factors control distinct processes in human neural stem cells](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39578497/)
summary: MEIS1 harbors the strongest known GWAS risk locus for restless legs syndrome (RLS) and encodes a homeodomain transcription factor expressed in the developing telencephalon and basal ganglia; intronic risk variants reduce enhancer activity in the ganglionic eminences and affect the dopaminergic system, linking early neurodevelopment to a neurological movement disorder with neuropsychiatric comorbidities. Functional studies in human neural stem cells show that MEIS1 and its paralog MEIS2 control distinct transcriptional programs, with MEIS1 directly regulating additional RLS-associated genes including NTNG1, MDGA1, and DACH1. (well-established)
